Top performing Reels
  1. [This is not a judgmental rant, but an attempt to share important information about the Husky breed and their tendency to escape, especially when they first leave the shelter system. Change creates stress for dogs. When they exit a shelter, they may have experienced days, weeks, or months of constant barking, close quarters with unfamiliar dogs, limited human interaction, and lack of exercise. Though we know they're heading to a better life when they're adopted, they may still face stress and uncertainty. Add in Huskies' notorious ability to escape, and you can have a challenging situation. Our primary goal is to save their lives, but keeping them safe is equally important. Please consider the following steps: * Ensure the dog is wearing an ID tag with your address and phone number. * Attach an AirTag to the collar for additional tracking. Although not foolproof, they can be helpful if the dog gets lost. Details on where to buy them are below. * Register the microchip immediately. * Educate the foster family about the tendencies of Huskies to bolt out doors, dig under or jump over fences, and escape from crates. * Walk the dog with a slip lead and a secure backup connection to a collar, as dogs can and do slip out of harnesses and flat collars. * Have the foster family walk the dog around the neighborhood rather than far from home. This way, if the dog escapes, they are more likely to find their way back. I purchase my AirTags from Amazon, where they sell individual tags and 4-packs. For holders, I use Saker Waterproof AirTag Holders designed for flat collars. Amazon carries these as well. If your rescue budget doesn’t allow for this, please DM me and I can send one to you. #rescuedog #husky #dogsafety](https://www.instagram.com/p/C6wzLqmSc9Z/)
35k
  2. [Six days ago, my heart was shattered when I woke to find Princess George unable to stand, paralyzed from the waist down. Thankfully, my friend @capo.dei.cani was visiting, and together, we managed to get her to the vet. X-rays revealed a devastating truth: her entire spine had degenerated and was severely compressed. Surgery was not an option. After 5 days, when she remained unresponsive to steroids and fluid began to build in her lungs, I faced the unbearable decision to let her go. Princess George was only five years old, a mere fraction of the time she deserved to have. She was perfect with all dogs and people, never showing anything but pure love to everyone she encountered. When she first arrived four years ago, she was completely feral, yet over time, she transformed into a loving member of my pack. George adored her pack, Aunt Betsy, and me with an unwavering loyalty and affection that knew no bounds. Her journey from a wild spirit to a beloved companion was a testament to her incredible heart and resilience. The bond we shared was irreplaceable, and her loss has left a void so deep that I struggle to find my footing in this new reality. I am not okay. The pain of losing her is a weight that seems impossible to bear. Princess George deserved many more years of happiness and love, and I would give anything to have granted her that. She was more than just a dog; she was family, a source of joy, and pure, unconditional love. Princess George, I love you with all my heart. Your memory will never leave my soul. I’d give anything to bring you back. Rest in peace, my sweet girl. You are already missed so much. I will never forget you.](https://www.instagram.com/p/C727duCxPX_/)
32.6k
  3. [Imagine if every time someone introduced us, they brought up a tragic event from our past. It would shape how others see us—as emotionally wounded or needing pity. We’d be treated differently, not as who we are now but as someone defined by that story. That’s exactly what happens when we constantly talk about our dog’s rescue story. While dogs live in the moment and let go of their past, we often hold onto it for them. They’re living their best life now, yet we’re stuck focusing on what happened before. The issue with this mindset is that it leads us to pity rather than rehabilitate and fulfill our dogs. If our dog has behavioral issues that could be resolved with structure and guidance, we might hesitate to provide it because of what we think they’ve been through. But even if a dog has faced neglect or abuse, why withhold the structure, guidance, and leadership they need to thrive? If I’m going through a tough time, I’d rather have my friends help me move forward than pity me. Let’s do the same for our dogs. Looking to strengthen your relationship with your dog? Join me and my community in our amazing virtual membership program, The Learn to Lead Academy! You’ll get live access to me four times a month, where we’ll tackle challenges and celebrate successes together. DM me “ACADEMY” for more info on how to join! #dogsofinstagram #doglover #dogpsychology](https://www.instagram.com/p/C_aulEIJS7s/)

  3. [First you create your habits then your habits create you The power of the mind has a major contribution to our abilities and are a key factor when tapping into our fitness potential. Be honest with yourself about your habits and set achievable goals that push you. You can 🙌 Looking good doesn’t always mean your moving right , but moving right often means your looking good 🧠 me against me rn. The sacrifices that come with prep are just beginning. But I know with God’s help first and foremost, the help of those around me who God blessed me with, and a strong mindset help fuel the driving force within. We are ALL stronger than our minds limit us to be. #gymfits #gym #fitness #workout #fit #fitnessmotivation #bodybuilding #motivation #gymmotivation #training #gymlife #muscle #fitfam #health #lifestyle #sport #Godislove](https://www.instagram.com/p/C33Rwfnyv8Z/)
